label: Palace of Westminster
sub: Seat of Parliament since 1265; current building rebuilt after 1834 fire. Home of the House of Commons and House of Lords.

label: Runnymede — Magna Carta (1215)
sub: Meadow where King John sealed Magna Carta on 15 June 1215, establishing that the king is subject to the rule of law.

label: Battle of Hastings (1066)
sub: Senlac Hill, near Hastings, Sussex. William the Conqueror defeated Harold II on 14 October 1066 — the last successful invasion of England.

label: Edinburgh Castle
sub: Seat of Scottish power; the Scottish Parliament was established nearby at Holyrood in 1999, restoring devolved government after 292 years.

label: Stormont — Northern Ireland Assembly
sub: Seat of the Northern Ireland Assembly established by the Good Friday Agreement (1998). Power-sharing between unionist and nationalist parties.

label: Senedd — Welsh Parliament, Cardiff
sub: Wales gained devolved government through the Government of Wales Act 1998. The Senedd acquired full primary legislative powers in 2011.

label: Bosworth Field (1485)
sub: Site of the final battle of the Wars of the Roses, 22 August 1485. Henry Tudor defeated Richard III, founding the Tudor dynasty.

label: Manchester — Cradle of the Industrial Revolution
sub: The world's first industrial city; the Manchester-Liverpool Railway (1830) and cotton mills made Manchester the 'Workshop of the World'. The Free Trade Hall and Engels' The Condition of the Working Class (1845) origin.

label: Birmingham — City of a Thousand Trades
sub: Matthew Boulton and James Watt's Soho Manufactory produced steam engines here from 1775. Birmingham led innovation in iron, steel, and the Birmingham Political Union campaigned for the Great Reform Act (1832).

label: Coventry — Blitz, November 1940
sub: On 14–15 November 1940 the Luftwaffe devastated Coventry in one of the worst bombing raids of the Blitz, killing 568 civilians and destroying the medieval cathedral. The city became a symbol of civilian resilience.

label: Orgreave Coking Plant (1984)
sub: Scene of the violent 'Battle of Orgreave' on 18 June 1984 between miners and police. Police charges remain contested and uninvestigated. A key flashpoint of the Miners' Strike.

label: Tilbury Docks — Empire Windrush (1948)
sub: The MV Empire Windrush arrived here on 22 June 1948 carrying 492 Caribbean migrants. The Windrush Generation built post-war Britain; many were later wrongly deported under the 'hostile environment' policy.

label: Balmoral Castle — Death of Elizabeth II (2022)
sub: Queen Elizabeth II died at Balmoral Castle, Aberdeenshire, on 8 September 2022, aged 96, ending a 70-year reign — the longest in British history.

label: 10 Downing Street
sub: Official residence and office of the Prime Minister since 1735. Home to Walpole, Pitt, Disraeli, Churchill, Thatcher, Blair, and Starmer.

label: Bank of England
sub: Founded 1694; granted operational independence to set interest rates by Gordon Brown in 1997. Manages monetary policy, financial stability, and sterling. The 'Old Lady of Threadneedle Street'.

label: Yorkshire Coalfields
sub: Heart of British coal mining. The South Yorkshire coalfields were epicentre of the 1984–85 Miners' Strike. Over 170 collieries closed under Thatcher; the last deep coal mine in England (Kellingley) shut in 2015.

label: Trafalgar Square, London
sub: Built to commemorate the Battle of Trafalgar (1805). Site of major political demonstrations from anti-Vietnam rallies to Brexit marches and Black Lives Matter protests.

label: Glastonbury Festival
sub: Michael Eavis founded the festival in 1970 on Worthy Farm, Somerset. Now the world's largest greenfield music festival, reflecting British cultural soft power and civil society traditions.

label: Belfast — Good Friday Agreement (1998)
sub: The Belfast Agreement signed 10 April 1998 ended three decades of the Troubles. It established the Northern Ireland Assembly, North-South Ministerial Council, and decommissioning framework.

label: Aberfan — Coal Tip Disaster (1966)
sub: On 21 October 1966, a colliery spoil tip collapsed onto the village of Aberfan, killing 144 people, including 116 children. The NCB was found negligent. Queen Elizabeth II's delayed visit to Aberfan became one of her few publicly regretted decisions.

label: 7 July 2005 London Bombings
sub: Four coordinated suicide bombings struck London's public transport system on 7 July 2005, killing 52 civilians and 4 bombers. The deadliest terrorist attack on British soil since the Lockerbie bombing.

label: Lockerbie — Pan Am 103 Bombing (1988)
sub: Pan Am Flight 103 was blown up over the Scottish town of Lockerbie on 21 December 1988, killing 270 people. Libya was found responsible in 2001. The deadliest terrorist attack in UK history until 2005.

label: Oxford — Rhodes Scholarship & Imperial Legacy
sub: Oxford University (est. c. 1096) and Cambridge University (est. 1209) educated British imperial and political elites. Cecil Rhodes's endowment funds global scholarships; the 'Rhodes Must Fall' campaign (2015–) debates colonial memory.

label: Glasgow Clydeside Shipyards
sub: Clydeside built the world's greatest fleet of ships at peak Empire. 'Red Clydeside' (1915–1919) saw labour radicalism; the yards built the Queen Mary and Queen Elizabeth. Deindustrialisation from the 1970s devastated the region.

label: Channel Tunnel — UK-EU Link
sub: The Channel Tunnel opened 6 May 1994, linking Folkestone, Kent with Coquelles near Calais. Post-Brexit, irregular Channel crossings by small boats became a major political controversy, with over 45,000 arrivals in 2022.
